one_line_takeaway: PORTGPT shows that giving an LLM agent human-workflow-inspired tools (git history/trace, symbol location, compile-feedback loops) rather than pure in-context prompting substantially improves automated CVE patch backporting success (89.15% on prior benchmarks, 62.33% on a new harder multi-language dataset, and 9 patches merged into the real Linux kernel), markedly outperforming rule-based tools like FIXMORPH and TSBPORT, especially on complex, cross-language and long patches.
key_finding: On the combined prior-works dataset (1,815 cases) PORTGPT achieves 89.15% overall success (1618/1815), vs. TSBPORT 87.59% (1589/1815), TSBPORT* 71.18% (1292/1815), FIXMORPH 24.63% (447/1815), and plain ChatGPT 30.63% (556/1815); PORTGPT's advantage is largest on the hardest categories, Type-III 80.43% vs. TSBPORT 75.00%, and Type-IV 54.49% vs. TSBPORT 46.38% / FIXMORPH 8.70%. On the paper's own harder 146-case dataset PORTGPT reaches 62.33% (91/146 with GPT-4o) overall vs. TSBPORT 34.78% (24/69, C-only comparison) and FIXMORPH 4.34% (3/69); on C++ it gets 53.57% (15/28) and on Go 69.39% (34/49), where FIXMORPH/TSBPORT cannot run at all (C-only tools). Across LLM backbones (Table 2) on the 146-case set: GPT-5 92/146 (63.0%), GPT-4o 91/146 (62.3%), Gemini-2.5-Flash 82/146 (56.2%), DeepSeek-v3 76 or 77/146 (~52.7%), Llama-3.3-70B only 24/146 (16.4%), attributed to Llama's weaker/incorrectly-formatted function calling. Ablation (Table 4) shows GitUtils removal drops overall accuracy to 54.79% (Type-IV collapses to 32.05%); LocateSymbol removal drops overall to 56.16%; removing both drops to 44.52%; disabling AutoFix drops overall to 51.37%; disabling CompileTest drops overall to 50.00% -- versus PORTGPT's full 62.33%. Efficiency (Table 5): average 60,649 input / 3,589 output tokens, $0.19 cost, and 166.58s per patch overall, scaling from Type-I ($0.06, 53.22s) to Type-IV ($0.27, 222.52s). Real-world study: 9/18 challenging Linux mainline-to-6.1-stable CVE backports succeeded and all 9 were reviewed and merged by kernel maintainers (vs. TSBPORT 2/18); for Ubuntu, 10/16 LTS-to-downstream patch pairs (10 CVEs) succeeded.
core_contribution: PORTGPT is an end-to-end LLM-agent (built on GPT-4o via LangChain) for automated patch backporting: migrating a mainline patch (often a security/CVE fix) to older stable/LTS or downstream branches. It shadows the manual human backporting workflow with two stages: (1) Per-Hunk Adaptation, where an LLM agent uses three custom tools -- ViewCode (retrieve code by ref/file/line-range), LocateSymbol (ctags-based symbol lookup across versions), and GitUtils (History: commit history filtered to the hunk's lines since the fork point; Trace: minimal-edit-distance based detection of code relocation/movement) -- to locate and transform each hunk, applying it via an ApplyHunk tool that auto-corrects common patch-format errors (corrupted patch spacing, non-existent file paths, context mismatches) and gives the LLM structured feedback for iterative refinement (Automated Context Correction after 3 failed attempts); and (2) Final Patch Combination, where the combined patch is applied and compiled via a CompileTest tool with a regex-based error-message parser, and the LLM iteratively repairs compile errors. The paper's own contribution is this agentic tool-augmented architecture plus a new 146-case evaluation dataset spanning C/C++/Go across 34 programs; it is explicitly contrasted with prior rule/template-based backporting systems (FIXMORPH -- AST-level rules; TSBPORT -- predefined semantic templates; PATCHWEAVE -- concolic execution) which are cited comparison baselines, not part of PORTGPT itself.
threat_model: Not applicable in the adversarial-attacker sense -- this is a software-engineering automation paper, not an attack/defense security paper. The 'threat' addressed is maintenance risk: legacy/LTS branches remaining vulnerable because manually backporting mainline security patches (many tied to CVEs) is labor-intensive and error-prone, so patched code in mainline can lag unpatched in stable/downstream branches. The paper does not model an adversary attacking PORTGPT itself, nor prompt-injection/poisoning risks to the agent; its closest safety consideration is a 'Validation Chain' (Sec. 5) that runs the original PoC exploit against the patched version, functionality test suites, and (when available) a directed fuzzer (SyzDirect) to check that PORTGPT-generated patches neither leave the vulnerability unfixed nor introduce new bugs -- the paper reports 'No security risks were identified during 4 hours of fuzzing' on successfully backported Linux patches that differed from ground truth.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion (Sec. 7) states PORTGPT cannot reliably explain/interpret why a backported patch is correct (LLMs lack robust semantic understanding), so outputs should be treated as suggestions requiring manual review before applying to production code. LLM context-length limits are mitigated by per-hunk processing in Stage 1, but this can lose cross-hunk context from the original patch, potentially hurting accuracy. Performance degrades monotonically as patch/hunk size grows (Table 3: e.g., PORTGPT 94.5% on 1-5 line patches down to 28.6% on 200+ line patches on prior datasets). C++ Type-IV performance is notably weaker (5/17, 29%) than other languages/types, which the authors attribute to a small evaluation sample and higher average change size (~100 lines) for C++ failures. Ablation costs (four configurations x ~2000 cases) were judged 'prohibitive' in dollars, so ablations were run only on the smaller 146-case dataset, not the full 1,961-case corpus. Several relevant prior tools (SKYPORT, Mystique, PPathF, PATCHWEAVE) were excluded from comparison due to domain mismatch (PHP-only), function-level/perfect-localization assumptions, or PoC/scalability requirements -- so PORTGPT is not compared against every plausible baseline. Validation of correctness beyond ground-truth comparison relies on manual triple-review by researchers plus PoC/fuzzing only 'where available,' meaning some accepted patches may lack independent dynamic verification.

datasets_used: Self-collected new dataset (146 cases, 34 programs, C/C++/Go, CVEs from past 4 years)
16 Ubuntu patch pairs / 10 CVEs (Bionic, Focal, Jammy; LTS-to-downstream)
quantitative_results: Overall success on 1,815-case prior dataset: PORTGPT 89.15% (1618/1815) vs TSBPORT 87.59% (1589/1815) vs TSBPORT* 71.18% (1292/1815) vs FIXMORPH 24.63% (447/1815) vs ChatGPT 30.63% (556/1815)
Type-IV on prior dataset: PORTGPT 54.49% (188/345) vs TSBPORT 46.38% (160/345) vs FIXMORPH 8.70% (30/345)
146-case new dataset overall: PORTGPT 62.33% (91/146, GPT-4o); C-only subset PORTGPT 60.87% (42/69) vs TSBPORT 34.78% (24/69) vs FIXMORPH 4.34% (3/69)
C++ subset: PORTGPT 53.57% (15/28); Go subset: PORTGPT 69.39% (34/49)
Ablation overall accuracy on 146 cases: full PORTGPT 62.33%; w/o GitUtils 54.79%; w/o LocateSymbol 56.16%; w/o both 44.52%; w/o AutoFix 51.37%; w/o CompileTest 50.00%
Efficiency: overall avg 60,649 input tokens, 3,589 output tokens, $0.19 cost, 166.58s per patch (Type-I: $0.06/53.22s -> Type-IV: $0.27/222.52s)
Real-world Linux mainline-to-6.1-stable: PORTGPT 9/18 succeeded and all 9 merged by kernel maintainers vs TSBPORT 2/18 (11.1%)
Real-world Ubuntu LTS-to-downstream: PORTGPT 10/16 patch pairs succeeded
Patch-size sensitivity (prior datasets): PORTGPT 94.5% success on 1-5 line patches down to 28.6% on 200+ line patches; FIXMORPH near 0% beyond 30 lines
baselines_compared: TSBPORT* (TSBPORT variant without ground-truth target-file access, fairer comparison)
Plain ChatGPT in-context-learning baseline (numbers reported by TSBPORT paper)
PATCHWEAVE mentioned but excluded from direct comparison (concolic execution, requires PoC, scalability issues)
SKYPORT and Mystique/PPathF mentioned but excluded (PHP-only / function-level localization assumptions)
